question: ‘Land-and-expand’ in a strong dev-tool motion most reliably combines:
options: Organic usage that proves value, team adoption that consolidates norms and budget, and enterprise governance that makes Security/IT a partner.
A signed enterprise contract before any developer has used the product.
A press release before any pilot.
Free t-shirts at every quarterly business review.
explanation: Strong land-and-expand stitches three layers: real usage, real team standardization, and real governance partnership — none of which is optional at enterprise scale.

question: Which of the following is the most credible structure for a Cursor ROI story?
options: ‘Pick a wild number and round up.’
Baseline cost of engineering time → measurable workflow lever → conservative improvement hypothesis → risk-adjusted implementation.
‘Trust me, it pays for itself.’
‘Ignore baseline; just compare to last quarter’s revenue.’
explanation: ROI travels when it is structured: baseline you can defend, lever the buyer believes in, range that respects uncertainty, and a credible plan to get there.

question: ‘Promotional stat’ should be treated as:
options: A contractual commitment.
A hypothesis to validate in the account’s context until it is proven there.
A guarantee against any future variance.
Equivalent to a peer-reviewed academic citation.
explanation: Marketing stats start as hypotheses. Validate them in the buyer’s workflow before letting them carry the deal — and never treat them as contracts.

question: ‘Risk-adjusted implementation’ in an ROI narrative typically includes:
options: Training, guardrails, and an evaluation design that the buyer can defend internally.
A guarantee of zero behavior change required from any engineer.
A commitment to skip Security review entirely.
A promise that no policy will need to be updated.
explanation: ‘How will the org actually adopt this safely?’ — training, guardrails, and an explicit evaluation plan answer that question and protect the credibility of the savings number.

question: Which is the strongest opening question with a CISO or senior security stakeholder?
options: ‘What controls do you require for any tool that touches code or production data, and what audit evidence do you expect to see at scale?’
‘Have you ever used a Mac?’
‘What’s your favorite snack?’
‘Do you use light or dark mode?’
explanation: Open with their job: required controls and audit evidence at scale. That posture earns you the right to discuss specifics afterward.

question: MEDDPICC ‘Metrics’ for a Cursor deal are best chosen as:
options: Numbers the seller likes that may or may not match the buyer’s KPIs.
Outcomes the buyer already owns and measures — engineering throughput, incident rates, onboarding time, developer satisfaction signals.
Whatever marketing copy says is biggest.
A pre-printed list with no buyer input.
explanation: Metrics travel only when they are the buyer’s — pick the ones they already own and measure, not the ones that look best in your pitch.

question: MEDDPICC ‘Economic Buyer’ in a Cursor enterprise motion is most often:
options: An end-user developer who really likes the IDE.
Engineering leadership, with the CTO entering earlier in strategic accounts.
The receptionist on the way to the meeting room.
The cleaning crew.
explanation: Economic buyers tend to live in engineering leadership for dev tools, with the CTO showing up sooner when the deal is strategic platform-level.

question: MEDDPICC ‘Decision Criteria’ in a dev-tool deal usually includes:
options: Only price.
Security requirements and developer experience requirements — both matter, and both must be satisfied to standardize.
Whether the vendor sponsors a sports team.
The vendor’s social media follower count.
explanation: Both gates must clear: governance and developer experience. Winning one and losing the other does not produce an enterprise standard.

question: MEDDPICC ‘Paper Process’ for an enterprise Cursor deal typically includes:
options: Procurement, legal, data processing terms, and vendor risk reviews.
Stationery selection.
Choosing a font for invoices.
Picking the office holiday card.
explanation: Paper process is the unglamorous critical path: procurement mechanics, legal redlines, DPA terms, and vendor risk review. Map it early or lose the quarter.

question: Which is the most credible behavior in a security conversation?
options: Confirm claims against primary trust/security documentation, and never bluff in front of a CISO.
Promise any feature the CISO names, on the call, in writing.
Skip the Security conversation until the contract is signed.
Tell the CISO they don’t need to read the trust center.
explanation: Sourcing discipline beats improvisation every time. Bluffing once with a CISO usually ends the deal — and the relationship.

question: ‘Make expansion feel inevitable’ best translates to:
options: Hiding usage data so leadership has to guess.
Surfacing organic usage and value clearly so leadership’s natural next step is to govern and standardize, not debate whether to keep using.
Forcing a contract before any usage exists.
Asking Security to step out of the conversation.
explanation: When the org has already proven value, your job is to make standardization the obvious next move and to make Security a partner in how it happens — not an obstacle.

question: ‘Conservative improvement hypothesis’ in ROI usually beats a precise number because:
options: Buyers do not understand precision.
Defensible ranges withstand scrutiny better than false precision and protect credibility through implementation.
Ranges are required by law.
Single numbers always look unprofessional.
explanation: Ranges acknowledge uncertainty while still pointing to value. False precision invites a fight you don’t need — and burns you when results land within the range.

question: ‘Engineering throughput’ as a Cursor metric is strongest when it is paired with:
options: A vibe.
A second metric like incident rates, rework, or cycle time so improvement can be triangulated rather than gamed.
Marketing slogans.
Conference attendance counts.
explanation: Throughput alone can be gamed (more PRs, lower quality). Pair it with a quality or cycle-time signal so the story holds up under serious scrutiny.

question: Which is the most credible Cursor onboarding-time hypothesis to introduce in discovery?
options: ‘New engineers will be productive in zero days.’

‘Teams using strong repo-aware AI assistance often see meaningful ramp-time compression for new engineers — would you like to design a small evaluation to see whether the same shape holds for your most complex service?’
‘Onboarding is irrelevant.’
‘Onboarding always takes the same time, regardless of tooling.’
explanation: Frame as a hypothesis the buyer co-validates against their hardest service. That respects engineering culture and produces evidence that survives leadership scrutiny.

question: MEDDPICC ‘Champion’ in a Cursor deal should ideally:
options: Be willing to take introductions, restate your value internally, and tell you when something is not landing.
Be a recent acquaintance who hasn’t actually used the product.
Be anonymous to protect their identity.
Be the person with the smallest org influence available.
explanation: Real champions sell internally and give you honest signal. Without those two behaviors, you have a contact, not a champion — and the deal will lurch.

question: ‘Identify Pain’ in MEDDPICC for a Cursor deal works best when expressed as:
options: A vague reference to ‘productivity.’
A named workflow loss (review latency, onboarding ramp, multi-file refactor cost) tied to a measurable cost or risk the buyer already feels.
A complaint about a competing product.
A demand for a discount.
explanation: Pain that is specific to a workflow and tied to a number gets prioritized; abstract pain quietly slips off the roadmap.

question: ‘Competition’ in MEDDPICC for an existing Copilot account most often means:
options: A competing snack vendor.
An incumbent or alternative AI coding tool whose strengths and limits must be honestly mapped against the buyer’s real workflow.
An old version of the buyer’s git host.
An unrelated SaaS product.
explanation: Competition includes incumbents like Copilot. Treat it as honest mapping — strengths and limits against the buyer’s real workflow — not as a fight.

question: Which security topic is appropriate to escalate to a Cursor specialist?
options: Detailed contractual data-handling terms a CISO is preparing to redline.
Whether the IDE has a dark mode.
Which keyboard shortcut opens chat.
Whether the website uses a serif font.
explanation: When the conversation reaches contractual data handling and CISO redlines, bring in the specialist. Strategic credibility is built on knowing when to escalate.

question: Which behavior is most likely to convert grassroots Cursor usage into a governed standard?
options: Surfacing usage to leadership, partnering with Security on a control framework, and aligning Procurement on co-term and rollout.
Hiding usage from Security to preserve the champion.
Forcing standardization before usage exists.
Skipping Procurement until renewal.
explanation: Standardization needs sponsorship, security alignment, and procurement mechanics moving in parallel. Skipping any one causes stalls or surprises at the worst time.

question: ‘Decision Process’ in MEDDPICC for a Cursor deal most usefully maps:
options: The path from current state to a signed enterprise standard, including who decides what, in what order, by when.
Which color the contract is printed on.
The vendor’s social media calendar.
Office party planning timelines.
explanation: A decision process map is your antidote to surprises. Without it, the quarter ends with ‘legal needs another two weeks’ — every time.

question: Which posture is most credible when a buyer asks about ‘incident recovery time’ as a Cursor lever?
options: Promise a specific recovery-time reduction without any data.
Acknowledge it is a real lever, ask how the org currently measures MTTR, and propose a measurement plan that fits their incident process.
Refuse to discuss incident metrics.
Quote an unrelated industry benchmark as fact.
explanation: Incident metrics are an excellent ROI angle, but they are noisy and political. Anchor to how this org actually measures, then propose a defensible plan.

question: ‘Developer satisfaction’ as a metric becomes meaningful when:
options: It is captured the way the org already runs developer surveys, with explicit baselines and comparison cohorts.
It is anchored to a single tweet from one engineer.
It is announced in a press release.
It is quoted from a competitor’s marketing page.
explanation: Developer satisfaction is real signal but only when measured in the org’s existing methodology with proper baselines — otherwise it is anecdote.

question: ‘Sequencing the deal’ in a multi-stakeholder Cursor enterprise motion typically means:
options: Deciding which proof, in which order, lands with which stakeholder so the deal moves forward without contradicting itself.
Letting Procurement run discovery.
Sending the same email to every stakeholder.
Skipping engineering leadership.
explanation: Strategic sellers run a sequencing plan. Workflow proof for engineers, governance proof for Security, commercial proof for Procurement — all consistent, all timed.

question: ‘Implication of pain’ in MEDDPICC for a Cursor deal is best stated as:
options: ‘Things are slow.’
‘If this workflow does not improve, here is the named cost — slower releases, higher rework, harder hiring — and what that translates to for this org over the next year.’
‘We will lose customers tomorrow.’
‘Your competitors are winning.’
explanation: Implication is where pain becomes business case. Name the workflow cost and project it forward in the buyer’s language — not generic doom.

question: Which behavior most reliably builds long-term trust with a Cursor enterprise account?
options: Over-promise on every Security and ROI question to keep momentum.
Be precise with sources, honest about uncertainty, structured about ROI, and consistent across stakeholders — even when it slows individual calls.
Avoid Security and Procurement until contract signature.
Treat every promotional stat as contractual.
explanation: Strategic trust is built call by call: precise sourcing, honest uncertainty, structured ROI, and consistent stories across stakeholders. That is what buys you the next renewal — and the next account.
